title: Puppy
blurb: A friendly little dog. One of the first Korean words children learn at home.
fact: Korean families often call all small dogs gang-a-ji, even when they grow up!

title: Butterfly
blurb: A tiny flying flower. You'll see butterflies in every Korean kids' song.
fact: The Korean song 'Nabi-ya, nabi-ya' is over 100 years old.

title: Full Moon
blurb: The brightest moon of the year shines on Chuseok, Korea's harvest festival.
fact: Families share songpyeon rice cakes shaped like little half-moons.

title: Ramyeon
blurb: Steaming noodles in a red-hot bowl. The most-loved snack in Korea.
fact: Koreans eat over 70 packs of ramyeon per person each year.

title: Rose of Sharon
en: Rose of Sharon — Korea's national flower
blurb: A pink-and-white flower that blooms all summer long.
fact: Its name means 'flower that never falls' — a symbol of Korea itself.

title: Bap
blurb: In Korea, a meal isn't a meal without rice in the middle of the table.
fact: 'Did you eat bap?' is how Korean grandparents say 'How are you?'

title: Sagwa
blurb: Big, red, and crunchy. Korean apples are famous for being extra sweet.
fact: The same sa-gwa sound also means 'I'm sorry' in Korean.

title: Doll
blurb: From handmade fabric dolls to K-pop figures, Korea loves its dolls.
fact: Old Korean palace dolls were made of straw and paper.

title: Dragonfly
blurb: Speedy, see-through wings that zip around summer ponds.
fact: Korean kids in old villages used to chase them with paper nets.

title: Book
blurb: Korea loves books so much it has Book Festivals every year.
fact: Hangul was made so anyone could read a book — even kids.

title: K-pop
blurb: Big songs, big dances. K-pop is how the world hears Korean every day.
fact: The first K-pop song was sung in 1992.

title: Taegeukgi
blurb: A red-and-blue swirl with four black symbols. Korea's flag.
fact: The swirl in the middle means 'yin and yang' — balance.

title: Paengi
blurb: Wooden tops that Korean kids spin on icy ground in winter.
fact: Korean paengi can spin for over five minutes when whipped right.

title: Tiger
blurb: Korea's old folk tales all start with 'Long ago, when tigers smoked tobacco...'
fact: Korea's shape on a map is said to look like a tiger.

title: Arirang
en: Arirang — Korea's most loved folk song
blurb: A song about missing home. Every Korean knows the first line.
fact: UNESCO listed Arirang as a piece of world heritage in 2012.

title: Yagu
blurb: Korea's loudest sport. Fans bring drums and cheer for nine innings straight.
fact: Korean baseball stadiums have official 'chant leaders' on the field.

title: Eomuk
blurb: A warm winter snack you buy on the street, served with hot broth.
fact: On cold days, the broth cup is the best part.

title: Fox
blurb: Foxes in old Korean tales are clever and a little bit magic.
fact: A nine-tailed fox is called 'gumiho' and is famous in K-dramas.

title: Ori
blurb: Brown ducks waddle around Korean ponds. They love rice scraps.
fact: Old Korean weddings used wooden ducks as a love-symbol.

title: Dragon
blurb: Korean dragons live in rivers and bring rain when the land is dry.
fact: They don't breathe fire — they whirl water and wind.

title: Usan
blurb: Korea's rainy season lasts a month — every kid has a yellow umbrella.
fact: Old Korean umbrellas were made of oiled paper and bamboo.

title: Yutnori
en: Yut — a traditional New Year's board game
blurb: Four wooden sticks instead of dice. Whole families play it together at Seollal.
fact: The four sticks make five different scores — like a wooden surprise.

title: Geune
en: Swing — like the ones at a playground
blurb: Korean girls in old paintings ride giant swings during the Dano festival.
fact: The higher you swing, the better the wish for the year.

title: Ibul
en: Blanket — a warm Korean sleeping cover
blurb: Korean kids sleep on the floor under bright, padded blankets called ibul.
fact: Grandparents fold ibul into neat stacks every morning.
